title: Puri

overview.description: Small, puffed Indian breads made from wholemeal atta dough and deep-fried until golden and inflated.

method: Mix the atta and salt in a large bowl.
Add the oil or ghee and rub it into the flour.
Gradually add the water, mixing until a firm but pliable dough forms.
Divide the dough into 12 equal portions.
Roll each portion into a smooth ball.
Roll each ball into a small, thin circle approximately 10–12cm in diameter.
Carefully slide one puri into the hot oil.
Gently press the surface with a slotted spoon to encourage it to puff.
Once puffed and golden underneath, turn and fry briefly on the other side.
Remove and drain on kitchen paper.

learning.did-you-know: Puri is eaten throughout India and can be served with savoury curries, vegetables and dal, as well as sweet preparations in some regions.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How wholemeal atta dough can be rolled and deep-fried to create a light, puffed bread.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A classic Indian bread that is fun to make, introduces deep-frying and offers a practical alternative to sandwiches for packed lunches.
chapman-tips: Puri dough should be firmer than chapati dough; a softer dough can absorb more oil.
Roll the puri evenly so it puffs consistently.
The oil needs to be hot enough for the puri to puff quickly.
Press gently on the surface with a slotted spoon to help it inflate.
If the oil is too cool, the puri may become greasy rather than light.
Puri are at their best fresh, while still warm and puffed.
notes.batch-cooking: Puri are best eaten fresh and are not ideal for freezing once cooked. The dough can be prepared ahead and refrigerated for a short period.
notes.leftovers: Leftover puri can be eaten cold or gently reheated. They make a useful lunchbox bread with simple fillings or dips.
